does: This paper reveals and addresses a fundamental vulnerability in class-level machine unlearning where models suppress predictions of forgotten classes by simply reducing bias terms in the final classification head rather than genuinely unlearning class representations. The authors conduct gradient-flow analysis of classification-head biases under softmax cross-entropy training to explain why retain-set-only optimization naturally reduces biases of absent classes. They introduce BiasShift as a diagnostic baseline demonstrating that conventional unlearning metrics—retain-set accuracy, forget-set accuracy, and unlearning time—can be satisfied through shallow bias manipulation while leaving detectable patterns that expose forgotten classes. To address this shortcoming, they propose two bias-aware mitigation mechanisms including a Two-Stage Bias Gradient Reversal Mechanism designed to achieve genuine unlearning while maintaining balanced bias patterns and model utility.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature in machine unlearning that emerged from privacy and intellectual property concerns in deep learning, extending beyond earlier approximate unlearning methods that focused only on task metrics. It responds to limitations in existing class-level unlearning approaches that ignore internal model mechanisms, connecting to recent work examining unlearning through the lens of representation learning and feature erasure. The paper engages with fundamental understanding of how neural networks learn and forget, drawing on gradient-based analysis techniques from interpretability research. It extends evaluation methodology discussions in the unlearning community by challenging reliance on surface-level accuracy metrics and proposing diagnostic baselines that expose when models use shortcuts rather than genuine learning suppression.
enables: This work opens multiple research directions in trustworthy machine unlearning. It establishes the importance of diagnosing unlearning mechanisms beyond conventional metrics, enabling future work to develop more rigorous evaluation frameworks that validate genuine knowledge removal rather than metric satisfaction through shortcuts. The bias-aware mechanisms proposed provide a foundation for designing unlearning algorithms with built-in accountability—preventing models from hiding forgotten information in easily accessible parameters. This framework could extend to instance-level and feature-level unlearning where similar shortcut vulnerabilities likely exist. The paper also motivates deeper investigation of how different architectures and training objectives affect unlearning shortcuts, potentially leading to theoretical characterizations of what makes unlearning robust and verifiable for real-world deployment in privacy-critical applications.
